notes.anchor: If you are well, I am glad; I myself am well
notes.note: The Latin opens with the formula S. V. B. E. V. (si vales, bene est; valeo), "if you are well, it is well; I am well" --- the conventional, slightly old-fashioned health-greeting of a Roman letter. Cicero almost never uses it; its appearance here marks this as Lucceius's own letter to Cicero, not Cicero's prose.
Renders the closing vale, the standard sign-off of a Roman letter. Together with the opening health-formula it frames Lucceius's note in the plain conventional manner.

notes.anchor: you who in the very sharpness of your mind see what is most deeply hidden
notes.note: Lucceius gently turns Cicero's celebrated acumen against him: a man who perceives the hidden ought all the more to see the plain truth that grief accomplishes nothing. The letter is a friend's reproach to Cicero's withdrawal into mourning after Tullia's death.
